speaker: interviewer
text: It's 1995. The interview is being conducted in Saddle River, New Jersey. Sulia, you were just telling me about how you were taken off the rubble.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I started working in an office, translating. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The most important thing was that I got in touch with the partisans [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by hiding them in the attic. They would send me on errands in the ghetto. And just as the Chinese don't want a girl, believe me, a girl in the woods was a burden. She wasn't considered a soldier. And somebody had to ask for you to go out. But I heard that this Bielski, no matter who came, women, children, he accepted everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumors. This one said, that one said. And the people who came from the woods. They told me that even if a boy doesn't ask for you, and you will come to the underground , you will be accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, through them, I knew already which farmer to contact, which road to take. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What did I know about the surroundings of Nowogrodek? Nothing. But they told me which road to take and where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, the second action, we called it, [NON-ENGLISH] comes. In this [NON-ENGLISH], I am trying to get my sister out of the ghetto because she's working in the hospital. And I knew that over there, they'll take everybody and kill them. For Mamma and Papa, I still had hopes. Later I found out that my mother was put on the side of death. But there was a-- a big barrel from water. She turned it over and hid underneath. And when they went away, she crawled out and came and stood near-- next to my father. Rita, I pulled out to work with me and my sister. And from there, we were-- I tried to get a shine, which means that they need me to leave. And I got it for her and me. And we were transferred to the armory. And over there, when everybody from this [NON-ENGLISH] soldiers, Latvian soldiers, Germans at the top with the dogs. And I knew that this was dead. And again a segregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left, right, left, right. This time I saw that the left side was close to the buildings. My sister and I are near the woods. I said, Rita, we are going to die. She says, so what's the difference? Let it be sooner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: | pulled her through by force. They were shooting after us. And we're running into the line of life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were locked in the stables of-- of-- from horses for three days without water, without food, without water. And we heard the screams and the crying of the people near the graves, right in the back of the stable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And again, I shouldn't say it, but I'm going to say it. Men, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] male men, a male, finds a cure for pain is a woman. There were guys whose wife was-- wives were crying. They were trying to make passes with my sister and me. If they still function today, I don't know, because they got kicked in good places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their wives had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the children. And they were like animals. I don't know what it is, instinct or fear. They say that when people are hungry, they have an erection. Who knows what kind of an animal the human being is. We saw it already. We are-- we are people too. It's among us too, the instinct, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they try to rape you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I told you, that week. My sister and I kicked but well. I don't know who-- you had to be an animal on this-- on this horse's manure, without water, dirty, smelling, and it-- it just-- just unbelievable. And on the third day, the doors opened. A shining Nazi came in, if you ever saw a god, blonde, blue-eyed, with tall, but shining boots in this dirt. Saying to us, [NON-ENGLISH] We give you life to work and produce. You will stay alive, and you will work-- just like in Auschwitz-- the sign. Work makes life sweet. You will go, and you will work, and you will productive. Meanwhile, between the armory and the courthouse, where the-- it was the working camp, and the rest were left in the ghetto. They were later on burned, all of them. One of my uncles among them. We were chased three kilometers, beaten on the way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I came in running, the-- the door was open. And I was holding onto my sister. And the two of us came running. And suddenly, I saw mamma and papa. And mamma had the first grey hair. She had black hair, and she had the first-- and she saw my children. And they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had pails of water prepared for us, and we drank like horses, you know? Because we didn't have any water. And mamma said, don't drink too much, you'll have a belly ache. Drink little-- sip by sip. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at her, and I said, Mamma, today your daughter died. Today a killer was born. Anybody who stays in my way, I will kill first. I don't know how. But I will revenge. And I never could. I wasn't that way, anyway. If it's not in you, it's not in you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were taken out from the courthouse twice a day for water. There was no water, no water facilities. As I said before, Nowogrodek never had sewage. So we had no water. There was no river. We had to bring it from wells outside. And from one of those wells, I decided to run away. And there was a girl whom I knew. I pulled her with me. Somebody saw it. And they told them. And we were caught. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were caught. And for three days, we were kept by the Polish police. They didn't touch my friend, Jasia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they beat me. I don't know why. Maybe they knew that-- and the chief of the police was my parents' former patient. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have two dimples in my behind. They beat me so. They did not rape me. But they beat me. And evidently, they broke my tailbone. Because up to this day, I have a lot of trouble. It settled like it's arthritic. I was arthritic even at 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the third day, this [NON-ENGLISH] said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], do you want to stay here with us, or do you want to go to jail and go to the Nazis? And I looked him in the eye, and I said, if I have to be killed, I don't want to be killed by former friends. Give me to the Germans. And that was a certain death. But you see, if it's [NON-ENGLISH], if it's meant to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two Germans in that unit. One was Meister Miller, who was a killer. The other one was Meister Wolf, who is supposedly better. And at that day, he was there. And his secretary was a Polish boy by the name of Witek Kastanow, who was like two classes higher than I, whose life I later on saved, because he helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as they were interrogating me, this Meister Wolf, and I spoke in German. He says to me, and who is your mother? When I told him, he says, is she a little woman? I said, yes. He was my mother's patient in the first World War. And he still had the bridge that she put in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he sent out Vitek from-- and Vitek said, I swear for her that she didn't go to the partisans. I know her background. She doesn't know anything outside of the city. He said, OK. You go out. I'll speak to her. He knew I was going there. Because I'll tell you what he said to me at the end. He said, look. I'm afraid myself. I must send you to jail. But on the third day, when they'll take you out, all of you, on the trucks, to be shot, behind the armory, try to stay close to the tarp. So when I send somebody whom I can trust-- was this adjutant by the name of Boyd-- and he'll call out your name. You'll jump out. They'll stop. And you'll jump out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think I believed it? No. But he ordered a nurse to come and do something to my behind. She was so horrible. She-- you know, by this time, it dried in the blood, and it stuck. She tore it. And she says, I don't know why they'd let me do something to this trash. They'll shoot you anyway. What for? On the third day, when they took us out, and you look at these people, I always imagined a jail full of criminals. All I saw were Jews. And they wore the prayer shawls. And they were praying. Praying, and praying, and praying. And I saw skeletons. I said, what am I doing with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stood close to the tarp. I tried to be the last one and kept this girl back with me, this [? Yacca. ?] Wouldn't you know, she married the German, stays in Germany. Had I known, I would let her go. [LAUGHS] Oh, God. That was horrible. I pulled her off-- off with me later on. Suddenly, you hear, halt! They stopped. And somebody called, Sulia [INAUDIBLE]! Pulled her off with me. We come back to the workplace. The Jewish police wouldn't let us in. This German who risked his life brings these two girls, and the German police is afraid to let us in. The-- the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we called-- he called, Subocz, was a gentile who took her. He opened the gate. And I came in. By that time, the management of the work camp came and took away my winter coat so I wouldn't go any place and jeopardize their lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out in a sweater. I waited until my behind healed. My mother stood in the front. You know, they used to call appel. You know, call everybody and come. I had no number. I had a hole underneath of my bunk. And I would go and cover myself and lay there. The police would come look-- empty. They would go out. Because I used to say, from that appel Mama, they're going to take us and shoot us. I'm not going. And my mother used to go to my friends and say, influence my daughter. She's crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: A fly wants to fight against an elephant. What is she going to do in the woods? She'll freeze to death. I had four times pneumonia when I was a child. She said, she's a sickly child. How will she survive? Right? Oh, God. I ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks later, I ran in a sweater. I ran across to the woods. I stayed there. And as I stayed there, I look around. There were a few more people. One of them lives-- lives in Petah Tikva. One of the girls I ran with. And I knew the road. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Waited till the evening. We got out. I went towards-- he knew me-- towards Franek. Frank, he used to be the dog catcher. And I had a dog that I walked on Yom Kippur. I remember that dog. I gave him away to some gentiles with a sack on her head. Because she would bite every Nazi. [LAUGHS] One time we gave her away. She came back. So we put-- gave her-- gave her to somebody. It was one of the-- my mother's patients. And my mamma stood in the front door, and she wouldn't let me go. And I picked her up bodily. She weighed-- all her life she went to [INAUDIBLE] to lose weight. She was maybe size 3. She had Hitler's diet. She didn't eat anything. I moved her away. And I said, Mamma, don't stay in my way. Because if I go, maybe I'll pull you out. Here, I'll perish for-- for sure. And then we came. We had to go from Franek to a man by the name of Boinski, who had six-- six beehives, three on one side, three on-- that was the sign-- three on the other. And birches. And I came there. And I had to tell him that I am Sasha's wife. One of the partisans. They all changed names. So he was Sasha. That I'm Sasha's [INAUDIBLE], Sasha's wife. And I came there. And on the third day, he said, you have to go. The partisans are not coming. My neighbors know. And God knows what will happen to me. I have small children. But that evening, a knock at the door. And the youngest Bielski came in, Aharon, he was 14 years old. He took us out from there, with the other partisans. And me he took to a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] house where a sister of a former priest, and the priest was there too hiding out. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. He was hiding out the whole family [? Boldo. ?] And they were two of my school friends. And with them, I stayed about a month. After a month came an order that we all must go to the woods. Because the Germans-- we would sleep by day, travel by night. And we would make like 15, 20 kilometers a night. Surround the farm, even milking, she had to go with one of us, the woman. That nobody could go out. And next day, you would move someplace else. But I stayed with these people. And then when it came, the order. Because the Germans found out and send the local police. At that time, my commander's wife was killed, Sonia. Her sister Regina-- Regina was killed. Many fellows. And when I came to the first huts, bunkers, I met the first time, the commander. And he seemed to me seven feet tall. He was over six. But he was a giant of a man, with a mop of dark hair, blue-eyed, high, pink-- pink cheekbones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the guys said, you brought me another woman, another whore to feed? And I didn't know that this was an expression in Russian. So there was one of the friends of mine who was a commander's wife. I says, Sonia, what does it mean? She says, a whore? I said, but I'm not a whore. But why does he call me that? And this giant of a man said, don't you dare to say a word like that. How many girls have we left like her? As many as they are, they all come to me. And with me, they'll have a place. If I'll survive, they'll survive. And that was Tuvia Bielski. So the one who took me out-- two families helped me. Boldo and Bielski. If not Bielski. My husband could survive on his own because he was never locked up. He went from the first day onto the woods. But I would never survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielski family were-- their people had a meal in [INAUDIBLE]. They knew the woods. They all served in the Polish army. Tuvia, especially, was a man who spoke fluently Polish and Russian and was awesome. He somehow, this Jew, everybody respected. He had a presence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did these people find the thought of leadership? I have no idea. Certainly not from my parents' philosophies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where did they find the courage or the idea to save Jews? I don't know. But just as there are righteous gentiles, there are righteous Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: From when I came, we were about 280 people, and very few women. There was a brigade of 1,200 Jews. We were the suppliers. We build a shtetl in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had factories. I have my boots. Would you like to see them? I'll show it to you later. My boots were made by my fellows. We had-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. That means work places, work factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got up in the morning normally, like they had a job. Some went-- the fighters usually went in the evenings for supplies, to bring food in and fight. You had to mine trains. You have to go and mine a-- a police station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I looked around, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where does a person like me, brought like me, become shrewd? I don't know. It must be something innate. It must be from my mother, something. Because she was a pragmatist.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father never could ask a patient for money. She had to ask for it. Because to him, if you told him a story that you didn't have it, he didn't ask you for the money. She had two daughters to raise. And it came, that pragmatism, that I said, alone, I will survive. But I'll never have a gun. I stood guard. Yes, they told me how to load it and how to unload it, and how to shoot it. I stood guard with one guy who lives in Brooklyn. He's a friend of mine. He's much older. But he was half blind with glasses. His glasses were broken, and I'm near-sighted. If the Germans came upon us, we wouldn't see them. Believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had to learn how to cook at the bonfire. Were you ever in camp? They taught you how to make a bonfire? With dry wood. How do you make it with wet wood? I mean, I used to cry. I thought many times, I'm going to commit suicide. Where did that come up? I will not-- I will not learn to swim. I'll sink. I was a failure. I didn't know anything. Standing guard, and I looked probably like-- like-- Cinderella with ashes on her face. I didn't know anything. How did I know if you make something that you have to mix it? It burned. And if it burned, the fellows were angry. Because they had to risk their heads to bring-- bring the flour. And I stand there, and I don't know you have to mix it. They had to teach me to boil water. Oh, God.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt so useless, so unneeded. But I said, I must find a guy. To find a guy was very easy. But to find a guy that would protect you and stay with you, I was horribly afraid of pregnancy. Because I faced a few friends who had abortions, or had in the sixth month, an abortion. And this was already a foreign baby. And you had to bury it. And you didn't-- nobody raped you. Nobody forced you. Don't get me wrong. Nobody forced anybody. They tried. You couldn't blame a guy for trying. But they did not force. And then couples slowly got together. If one had got killed, she looked for another one. But without a man-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was survival. There were people who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you wanted to go hungry all the time, and if you wanted-- they called it a malbush. Malbush is a-- is a-- in Hebrew, it's a suit without the human being in it. OK? A piece of cloth. Nothing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never anybody's girl Friday. And I had to fight for my identity, even there. And I wanted to be a scout. I said, OK, I rode the horse in the park. But I'll learn how to ride the horse here. I did. And then one of the fellows from a new group, older than me, a lot older, was my husband's oldest brother. He called us my-- my-- my little sisters. And he would bring a piece of soap, a piece of bread. Because we had a kitchen. Every day, you had a plate of soup with a piece bread, a portion. And he said to me, listen I am not for you. First of all, I am an alcoholic. Second of all, I buried a wife and a child, and since then, I'm no good. But I have a brother who is like a year and a half, two years older than you. He'll be just right for you. I said, OK. Show me him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, I had two others. But I was afraid. Once you got a girl, the first thing you brought her was a sheepskin coat. That was like an engagement ring. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you were called the [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] means you belong to this guy. We developed our own language, with our own-- own-- you know, passwords. It wasn't easy to be Jewish-- Jewish partisans because we couldn't go alone. We have to go in groups, otherwise we were killed by the Russian partisans or by the Polish partisans. So-- so from all sides. But there is one thing about being in the partisans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You wake up and say, gee. If I carry a gun, my bullet could kill you just the same as yours could. You become proud. You suddenly drop the fear. You are not a number. And this is the difference between me and the-- and the-- my brothers and sisters from the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was an equal. I could kill too. They, poor things, were pushed in the oven. They couldn't do a thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And yet, I resented when they say to me, what did you do, all of you? How could you let yourself be pushed to an oven? I would like to see, by our experience-- in the States they have learned. But I would like to see if somebody would come to you or to me now, and say, gee. Skinheads will come. And they'll take you all. And they'll put you in a crematorium. Would you believe it? You would stay at home. Who believes in things like that? But thanks to the fighters, suddenly, we woke up. And we said to ourselves, yes. These Jews are different Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: You will take us, But we'll take one of you with us. It doesn't-- it gives you a completely different perspective. And this is thanks to the Bielski brothers and people like them. There was a [? Zorian ?], another one. There were about 65,000 partisans. And I must say, that we were supported by the Russians. That the Russians from Moscow, we had their radio, we had a base. And they said that anybody who kills a Jew will answer for it after the war as a criminal. They are partisans, and they are fighting just as good as you. They are equals.

speaker: interviewer
text: They send us, like, they send us sulfur. Right? I would stand guard on them with my husband, on the airport. We had a place where we would signal. They would come. Nobody else but the Russians gave it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, I took this sulfur in a linen cloth, crushed it. And it was a powder. Once it became a powder, you have to mix it with something-- lard from a pig. It's not pure, but lard from a boar. They are vegetarians. They eat roots. It's pure. We killed the boar. Melted the-- the fat, mixed it with the sulfur, and you cured with it everything-- frostbite, everything healed. If you had an infection, everything, it was a known salve that I made. And it went. I became a nurse too. And when my husband came to me-- when-- first he took me to see him. And he had, at that time, a swollen cheek. Something was wrong with a tooth. I took a look. I said, oh my God. I said, look. When you look normal, come and see me then. He didn't leave me alone. Till this day, I-- it torments me. Because had I said right away, yes, maybe he would save my sister and my mother-- they were still alive-- and my father. I was sending through-- through the pharmacy. I was sending them letters. I was sending them, I am free. You don't realize what it means to be in the woods. Come out. You have me. And if you'll tell me when, I will wait the crossing-- the woods across from the courthouse-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my husband. I debated. I thought maybe a prince and a white horse will come and swipe me off my feet. I was still romanticizing. And finally, when I said yes to him and came over to him April the 18th, my parents were shot, May the 7th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think if you--

speaker: interviewee
text: One letter he sent to them. And I'll tell you what my husband says to me. Did you wait for anybody to pull you out? You weren't on your own. They had you. They could have come out. They could have run like you did. They were afraid, and they didn't want to. You have nobody to blame for it. This was their fate, and they perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister came out. When I was going out, I said, Rita, come with me. She says, are you kidding? I'm not coming with you. I'm working as a cleaning woman for the police. If I'll come out, and I have to shorten Mamma's and Papa's life for one minute, I'm not going. You don't exist. You can go. I'll come later.
